schools.summary: The word of the LORD assures Abram: 'after these things the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision, saying, Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward' — God revealing himself to Abram in a vision (the first mention of 'the word of the LORD' coming to a man, the divine communication that opens the great covenant chapter, God drawing near to comfort and assure his servant after the war, ch. 14); God saying 'Fear not, Abram' (perhaps Abram feared the revenge of the defeated kings, or felt a reaction after the victory, God comforting him against fear, the assurance of the LORD to his servant, 'fear not,' the word of God to the believer); 'I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward' (God promising to be Abram's shield, his protection and defense, against all enemies, and his exceeding great reward, his portion and recompense, far more than the spoil of Sodom he had refused, 14:23, God himself, not the wealth of Sodom, Abram's reward, the LORD his protector and his portion, the believer's all in God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: After these things the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision, saying, Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward
the word of the LORD assuring Abram, 'I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward'
God himself, not the wealth of Sodom, Abram's protection and portion
the believer's all in God

schools.summary: Abram pleads his childlessness: 'and Abram said, Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?' — Abram, in the freedom of faith, laying his trouble before God: what good were God's gifts while he remained childless, without the heir of promise? (the patriarch's longing for the promised seed, the plea that, for all the promises, he had no child, the prayer of faith that lays hold of God's word and pleads the promise); Abram 'go(es) childless' (still without a son, despite the promise of a seed, 12:2, 7; 13:16, the long trial of his faith, the patriarch feeling the delay, yet bringing his complaint to God in faith, not in unbelief, the believer pleading the promise in his trial); 'the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus' (Eliezer, Abram's chief servant, steward, a Damascene, in default of a son, the prospective heir of Abram's house, the patriarch's household to pass to a servant, not a son, the want that pressed on Abram, the contrast with the promised seed, the occasion of God's renewed promise, vv.4-5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abram said, Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?
Abram's plea over his childlessness
the trial of the delayed promise of a seed (12:2)
the prayer of faith laying the trouble before God

schools.summary: Abram presses his complaint: 'and Abram said, Behold, to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir' — Abram pressing his complaint further: God had given him no seed (no child of his own), so that one born in his house (a home-born servant, Eliezer) must be his heir (the patriarch's want of an heir of his own body, the renewed laying of his trouble before God); Abram saying 'to me thou hast given no seed' (for all the promises, he having as yet no child, the long delay of the promised seed, the trial of faith pressing on the patriarch, yet his complaint the plea of faith, pressing the promise before God); 'one born in my house is mine heir' (in default of a son, Abram's home-born servant, Eliezer, inheriting, the patriarch's estate to pass to a servant, the want that occasioned God's express promise of a son of his own body, v.4, the contrast between the servant-heir and the promised seed) — Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And Abram said, Behold, to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir
Abram pressing his complaint that a servant must be his heir
the want of an heir of his own body, the trial of the promise
the servant-heir setting off the promised seed to come

schools.summary: God promises a son of Abram's body: 'and, behold, the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir; but he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir' — God answering Abram's complaint: the servant (Eliezer) would not be his heir (God setting aside the servant-heir, the LORD's word correcting Abram's fear and renewing the promise of a true seed); 'he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir' (God promising Abram an heir of his own body, a son born to him, not a servant, but a true child, the express promise of a son, fulfilled in Isaac, ch. 21, the renewal and clarifying of the seed-promise, the patriarch assured of a child of his own); God's word that Abram's own son would be his heir answering the patriarch's plea (vv.2-3, the promise of a true seed, against the human impossibility, Abram old, Sarai barren, the assurance that God would give what nature could not, the promise that called for and strengthened Abram's faith, v.6) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And, behold, the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir; but he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir
God's promise of a son of Abram's own body
the servant-heir set aside, a true son (Isaac) promised
the promise against the human impossibility, strengthening Abram's faith

schools.summary: The stars as the measure of the seed: 'and he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said unto him, So shall thy seed be' — God bringing Abram out (it being night, a vision, v.12) and bidding him look at the stars and try to count them (the innumerable stars set before Abram as a visible measure of his promised seed, the sign that strengthened his faith); the stars innumerable to man (as Abram could not number them, so his seed beyond numbering, the promise of an innumerable posterity, as the dust, 13:16, and now the stars, the vast multitude of his descendants, natural, Israel, and spiritual, all the faithful, Romans 4:18; Galatians 3:7, 29); 'So shall thy seed be' (as the stars, so numberless Abram's seed, the promise that against all human impossibility, the childless old man, God would give an innumerable posterity, the word that Abram believed, v.6, the seed of promise, as the stars of heaven, the children of faith) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said unto him, So shall thy seed be
God showing Abram the stars as the measure of his seed
the seed beyond numbering, as the stars (Romans 4:18)
the promise of an innumerable posterity to the childless patriarch

schools.summary: Calvin and the evangelical reading (justification by faith): 'and he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness' — Abram believing God's promise (of the seed, v.5, and so of the Messiah and all the covenant blessings, resting his faith on God and his word, trusting God who quickens the dead and calls things that are not as though they were, Romans 4:17, the faith of the patriarch, the pattern of all believing); God 'counted it (Abram's faith) to him for righteousness' — Abram's faith reckoned, imputed, accounted to him for righteousness (not that faith is itself a meritorious work, but that, by faith, the righteousness of God, ultimately the righteousness of Christ, is imputed to the believer, Abram justified, accounted righteous before God, not by works but by faith, Romans 4:3, 22; Galatians 3:6, the doctrine of justification by faith, apart from works, here founded, Romans 4, faith the instrument, not the ground, of justification, the righteousness reckoned God's gift, received by faith); a complementary reading (with James 2:23) noting that Abram's faith was a living, working faith, shown by his obedience, but the imputation of faith for righteousness, the free reckoning of righteousness to the believing sinner; the great gospel doctrine, that the sinner is justified by faith, not by works, the pattern of the justification of all who believe, Jew and Gentile, Romans 4:11-12, 23-24, Abram the father of the faithful — Gill, Calvin, Clarke, JFB.

debates.question: What does 'counted it to him for righteousness' teach about justification?

sides.position: Justification by faith apart from works — faith reckoned/imputed as the instrument by which God's (Christ's) righteousness is credited to the believing sinner (the evangelical/Reformed reading, Romans 4:3, 22)
The faith that is counted for righteousness is a living, working faith, shown by obedience (the complementary emphasis of James 2:23)

themes: And he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness
Abram's faith counted to him for righteousness (the foundation of justification by faith)
faith reckoned/imputed for righteousness, justification apart from works (Romans 4:3, 22)
the righteousness of God imputed to the believer, Abram the father of the faithful

schools.summary: The LORD who brought Abram out: 'and he said unto him, I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give thee this land to inherit it' — God declaring himself the LORD (Jehovah) who had called and brought Abram out of Ur (11:31; 12:1; Acts 7:2-4, the God who began the work would complete it, the ground of the promise the faithful God who had already led Abram thus far); God reminding Abram of his calling out of Ur (the God who brought him from his idolatrous homeland, Joshua 24:2, to the land of promise, the past mercy the pledge of the future, the faithful God who had begun would finish); 'to give thee this land to inherit it' (God's purpose in calling Abram to give him the land, Canaan, for an inheritance, the land-promise renewed, the same God who promised the seed, v.5, now confirming the land, the double promise of seed and land, the substance of the covenant, the assurance that Abram's seed would inherit the land) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he said unto him, I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give thee this land to inherit it
God's declaration as the LORD who brought Abram out to give him the land
the faithful God who called Abram from Ur (Acts 7:2-4) confirming the land-promise
the past mercy the pledge of the inheritance

schools.summary: Abram asks for a sign: 'and he said, Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?' — Abram asking for assurance, a sign or token, that he (his seed) would indeed inherit the land (the question of faith seeking confirmation, not of unbelief doubting the promise, the patriarch asking how he might know the promise sure, the request that God would answer by the covenant of the pieces, vv.9-17); Abram's question not the doubt of unbelief (he believed, v.6) but the desire of faith for a confirming sign (like the believer who, resting on the promise, yet seeks the seal, God graciously condescending to give the assurance, the faith that seeks and receives confirmation); Abram's request for a sign answered by God's solemn covenant (vv.9-17, the sacrifice, the divided pieces, and the passing of the smoking furnace and burning lamp between them, God confirming the land-promise by covenant, the assurance Abram sought given in the most solemn form) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said, Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?
Abram's request for a sign of the inheritance
the question of faith seeking confirmation, not of unbelief
the request answered by the solemn covenant of the pieces (vv.9-17)

schools.summary: The covenant sacrifices: 'and he said unto him, Take me an heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon' — God commanding Abram to take certain animals (a heifer, a she-goat, a ram, each three years old, and two birds) for the covenant sacrifice (the animals later used in the Levitical sacrifices, the materials of the solemn covenant God would make, vv.10, 17); the animals (the clean beasts and birds, fit for sacrifice) to be prepared for a covenant ceremony (the ancient mode of making a covenant by sacrifice, cutting the animals and passing between the pieces, God's covenants with man made by sacrifice, the sacrificial animals prefiguring the great sacrifice); 'three years old' (the animals in their full strength and prime, fit for the solemn sacrifice, the careful specification marking the importance of the covenant, the materials prepared, to be divided, v.10, for the covenant of the pieces) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And he said unto him, Take me an heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon
God's command to take the covenant sacrifices
the ancient mode of making a covenant by sacrifice
the animals prepared for the covenant of the pieces

schools.summary: Abram divides the sacrifices: 'and he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another: but the birds divided he not' — Abram taking the animals, cutting them in two (dividing them down the middle), and laying the pieces opposite each other (the ancient rite of covenant-making, in which the parties passed between the divided pieces, Jeremiah 34:18-19, the preparation for the solemn covenant); the pieces laid opposite each other, leaving a path between (the manner of the covenant-rite, in which to pass between the pieces was to invoke the like fate, to be cut in pieces, on the breaker of the covenant, the solemn, self-imprecatory form of the ancient covenant, the path between the pieces through which the covenant-maker would pass, v.17); 'but the birds divided he not' (the birds, the turtledove and pigeon, not divided, perhaps as being small, or by custom, or as the doves in sacrifice were not split, Leviticus 1:17, the two birds laid whole, perhaps one on each side, the careful following of the rite, the sacrifice prepared for the covenant ceremony) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another: but the birds divided he not
Abram dividing the sacrifices for the covenant
the ancient covenant-rite of passing between the divided pieces (Jeremiah 34:18-19)
the path between the pieces through which God would pass (v.17)

schools.summary: Abram drives away the birds of prey: 'and when the fowls came down upon the carcases, Abram drove them away' — birds of prey (vultures, etc.) descending on the divided carcasses, and Abram driving them off (the patriarch guarding the sacrifice from defilement until God should act, the watchful care of Abram over the covenant-offering); the birds of prey coming down on the sacrifice variously taken — as a hindrance Abram drove off, and (by some) as a symbol — of the enemies of Abram's seed (the Egyptians, or the nations) who would seek to destroy them, or of Satan and the foes of the covenant (Abram's driving them away figuring the protection of the covenant-people, the trial of Abram's patience as he watched and waited); 'Abram drove them away' (Abram, watching by the sacrifice, driving off the unclean birds, his patient vigilance through the day, awaiting God's covenant-sign, v.12, the sun going down, the faithfulness of the patriarch in guarding the offering, the believer's watchful patience as he waits on God) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And when the fowls came down upon the carcases, Abram drove them away
Abram driving away the birds of prey from the sacrifice
the birds taken by some as the enemies of Abram's seed
the patriarch's patient vigilance, guarding the covenant-offering

schools.summary: The deep sleep and the horror of darkness: 'and when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, an horror of great darkness fell upon him' — at sunset, a deep, supernatural sleep (tardemah, like that on Adam, 2:21) falling on Abram (the trance in which God would reveal the future and make the covenant, the divinely-induced sleep that prepared the patriarch for the revelation, vv.13-16, and the covenant-sign, v.17); 'an horror of great darkness fell upon him' (a great dread and darkness coming over Abram, the awe and terror of the divine presence, and, as the revelation would show, vv.13-16, a foreshadowing of the affliction and bondage of his seed in Egypt, the darkness figuring the coming trial, the solemn dread that accompanied the covenant-revelation); the horror of great darkness foreshadowing the dark affliction of Abram's seed (the bondage in Egypt, v.13, the dread that came on the patriarch prefiguring the trial of his descendants, yet the covenant, v.18, and the deliverance, v.14, following the darkness, the darkness before the dawn of the promise) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, an horror of great darkness fell upon him
the deep sleep and the horror of great darkness that fell on Abram
the supernatural trance for the covenant-revelation (cf. 2:21)
the darkness foreshadowing the affliction of his seed in Egypt (v.13)

schools.summary: The prophecy of the bondage: 'and he said unto Abram, Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them four hundred years' — God foretelling that Abram's seed would be strangers and slaves in a foreign land (Egypt), serving and afflicted (the prophecy of the Egyptian bondage, Exodus 1; Acts 7:6, the future of the chosen seed revealed to Abram); the seed would 'serve them, and they shall afflict them' (the harsh bondage and affliction of Israel in Egypt, the suffering of the chosen people before their deliverance, the trial appointed for the seed of promise); 'four hundred years' (the round number for the period of the sojourning and affliction, the four hundred years, or four hundred thirty, Exodus 12:40; Galatians 3:17, reckoned variously, from Abram's seed being strangers, or from the entry into Egypt, the appointed time of the bondage, the period after which the deliverance would come, v.14, the foretold duration of the affliction) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And he said unto Abram, Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them four hundred years
the prophecy of the bondage and affliction of Abram's seed
the Egyptian bondage revealed to Abram (Acts 7:6)
the appointed period of the sojourn and affliction (Exodus 12:40; Galatians 3:17)

schools.summary: The judgment on Egypt and the deliverance: 'and also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out with great substance' — God promising to judge (punish) the nation (Egypt) that would enslave and afflict Abram's seed (the judgment on Egypt, the plagues, Exodus 7-12, God's justice on the oppressors, the assurance that the bondage would end in the judgment of the enslaving nation); God himself judging Egypt — the oppressor of his people (the divine vengeance on those who afflicted the chosen seed, the comfort of the promise that God would not leave the bondage unavenged, the judgment that would accompany the deliverance); 'afterward shall they come out with great substance' (after the bondage and the judgment, Abram's seed coming out of Egypt with great wealth, the spoiling of the Egyptians, Exodus 12:35-36, the deliverance and enrichment of Israel, the promise that the affliction would end in liberation and abundance, the bondage turned to blessing by God's faithfulness) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out with great substance
the promise to judge the oppressing nation and bring out the seed with substance
God's judgment on Egypt (Exodus 7-12) and the deliverance with great wealth (Exodus 12:35-36)
the bondage ending in liberation, by God's faithfulness

schools.summary: Abram's peaceful death promised: 'and thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace; thou shalt be buried in a good old age' — God promising Abram a peaceful death ('go to thy fathers' implying a gathering to his ancestors, and, as some note, a continued existence beyond death, the soul gathered to the fathers, not merely the body buried, the patriarch dying in peace, before the affliction of his seed); 'go to thy fathers' suggesting the gathering of Abram to his people beyond death (the phrase implying a hope of immortality, the fathers being not annihilated but gathered, Abram dying in peace, in the favor and hope of God, the peaceful end of the man of faith); 'buried in a good old age' (Abram living to a great age and honorably buried, fulfilled, 25:7-8, Abram dying at 175, buried by Isaac and Ishmael, the promise of a long, full life and a peaceful death, the blessing of the patriarch, who would not see the affliction of his seed but die in peace and hope) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace; thou shalt be buried in a good old age
the promise of Abram's peaceful death and burial in a good old age
'go to thy fathers' implying a gathering beyond death, a hope of immortality
the patriarch's peaceful end (25:7-8), before the affliction of his seed

schools.summary: The return and the iniquity of the Amorites: 'but in the fourth generation they shall come hither again: for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full' — Abram's seed, after the bondage (v.13), returning to Canaan in the fourth generation ('the fourth generation' variously reckoned — four generations of the long-lived patriarchs, corresponding to the four hundred years, v.13, or the fourth generation of those who went down to Egypt, the return of Israel to the land under Joshua); 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full' the moral ground of God's forbearance and justice (the Amorites, the Canaanite nations, not yet so wicked as to be ripe for the judgment of dispossession, God, in his justice, not giving their land to Israel until their iniquity was full, complete, deserving of the judgment, the delay of four hundred years God's forbearance, giving the Canaanites space to repent or to fill up the measure of their sin, when their wickedness was full, God judging them by Israel's conquest, the moral ground of the conquest — not Israel's merit, but the Canaanites' ripened iniquity, Deuteronomy 9:4-5; Leviticus 18:24-28, the patience and the justice of God displayed); God's long forbearance with the Amorites (four hundred years) showing his patience, not willing that any should perish, yet his justice, when their sin was full, not sparing, the dispossession of the Canaanites a righteous judgment on a people ripe in wickedness — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What does 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full' display?

sides.position: God's forbearance and justice — he delays the conquest four hundred years, giving the Canaanites space, until their iniquity is full and ripe for the righteous judgment of dispossession (the moral ground of the conquest, Deuteronomy 9:4-5)

themes: But in the fourth generation they shall come hither again: for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full
the return in the fourth generation and the iniquity of the Amorites not yet full (the crux of God's forbearance and justice)
God delaying the conquest in patience until the Canaanites' sin was ripe for judgment
the moral ground of the conquest - the Canaanites' ripened iniquity, not Israel's merit (Deuteronomy 9:4-5)

schools.summary: The smoking furnace and burning lamp: 'and it came to pass, that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp that passed between those pieces' — at full dark, a smoking furnace (or oven) and a burning lamp (a flaming torch) appearing and passing between the divided pieces of the sacrifice (v.10, the visible sign of God's presence, the symbols of the divine glory, the Shekinah, passing through the covenant-path); the smoking furnace and the burning lamp 'probably represented' the presence of God (the furnace, smoke, and the lamp, fire/light, figures of the divine presence, as the pillar of cloud and fire, Exodus 13:21, some seeing in the furnace the affliction of the seed, the iron furnace of Egypt, Deuteronomy 4:20, and in the lamp the light of deliverance, the symbols of God passing through the pieces); 'passed between those pieces' (God, in the symbols of the furnace and lamp, and not Abram, passing between the divided pieces, in the covenant-rite the party who passed between the pieces binding himself, invoking the like fate if he broke the covenant, here God alone passing through, binding himself by oath to Abram, the covenant of grace, in which God alone is the covenant-maker and surety, God pledging himself, unconditionally, to fulfill the promise, the wonder that God, condescending to the human form of covenant, bound himself with an oath, Hebrews 6:13-18) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

debates.question: What do the smoking furnace and burning lamp passing between the pieces signify?

sides.position: The symbols of God's presence (the Shekinah; the furnace perhaps the affliction of Egypt, the lamp the deliverance), God alone passing through to bind himself by oath in the one-sided covenant of grace (Hebrews 6:13-18)

themes: And it came to pass, that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp that passed between those pieces
the smoking furnace and burning lamp passing between the pieces (the crux of the covenant-sign)
the symbols of God's presence (the Shekinah; the furnace the affliction, the lamp the deliverance)
God alone passing through to bind himself by oath in the covenant of grace (Hebrews 6:13-18)

schools.summary: The covenant granting the land: 'in the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram, saying, Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates' — by the sign of the furnace and lamp (v.17), the LORD solemnly making (cutting) a covenant with Abram (the covenant of grant, confirming the promise of the land to Abram's seed, the formal, sworn covenant); 'Unto thy seed have I given this land' (God granting the land of Canaan to Abram's seed, the past tense, 'have I given,' marking the certainty of the gift, as good as done, the land-promise sealed by covenant, the inheritance assured to Abram's descendants); the land reaching 'from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates' (the boundaries of the grant, the 'river of Egypt' variously identified — the Nile, so some, or, more commonly, Clarke, the brook/wadi of Egypt, the Wadi el-Arish, the southern border, not the Nile; the extent, from the southern border to the Euphrates, read by some as the ideal boundaries, fulfilled at the height of Israel's kingdom under David and Solomon, 1 Kings 4:21, 24; 2 Chronicles 9:26, who reigned to the Euphrates, others noting that Israel never fully or permanently possessed the whole, the covenant-grant of the land, in its widest extent, to Abram's seed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What is the 'river of Egypt' and the extent of the land granted?

sides.position: The 'river of Egypt' is the brook/wadi of Egypt (Wadi el-Arish, the southern border, not the Nile); the extent the ideal boundaries fulfilled under David and Solomon (1 Kings 4:21)
The 'river of Egypt' is the Nile; the wide extent (Nile to Euphrates) an ideal never fully or permanently possessed by Israel

themes: In the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram, saying, Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates
the LORD's covenant granting the land from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates (the crux of the land's extent)
the 'river of Egypt' the Nile vs the brook/wadi of Egypt (Wadi el-Arish)
the extent fulfilled under David and Solomon (1 Kings 4:21) vs never fully possessed

schools.summary: The nations of the land (1): 'the Kenites, and the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites' — the first three of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed (the peoples whose territory was promised to Israel, the Kenites, a people later associated with Israel, the family of Jethro, Judges 1:16, the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites, eastern peoples, the nations to be dispossessed); ten nations named (vv.19-21), more than the usual seven (Deuteronomy 7:1, the fuller list marking the wide extent of the grant, to the Euphrates, v.18, embracing peoples beyond the borders of Canaan proper, the nations whose land was given to Abram's seed); the land of these nations granted by covenant to Abram's seed (the peoples whose territory Israel would, in part, possess, the wide extent of the promised inheritance, the nations of the land, named as the boundaries of the grant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: The Kenites, and the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites
the beginning of the list of the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed
the ten nations, more than the usual seven (Deuteronomy 7:1), marking the wide extent
the nations to be dispossessed

schools.summary: The nations of the land (2): 'and the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Rephaims' — three more of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed (the Hittites, the children of Heth, 10:15; 23:3, the Perizzites, a Canaanite people, 13:7, and the Rephaim, the giants, 14:5; Deuteronomy 2:11, the peoples whose land was promised to Israel); the Hittites and Perizzites among the chief Canaanite nations (often named in the lists of the peoples Israel would dispossess, e.g. 13:7; Exodus 3:8, the inhabitants of the land of promise, the nations to be driven out); 'the Rephaims' (the Rephaim, the ancient giant-people, 14:5; Deuteronomy 3:11, Og king of Bashan of the remnant of the Rephaim, the powerful, gigantic inhabitants of parts of the land, named among the nations whose territory was granted to Abram's seed, the wide extent of the grant embracing even the lands of the giants) — Gill.

themes: And the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Rephaims
the list of the nations of the land continued
the Hittites and Perizzites (chief Canaanite nations) and the Rephaim (giants)
the wide extent of the grant embracing even the lands of the giants

schools.summary: The nations of the land (3): 'and the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Girgashites, and the Jebusites' — the last four of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed (the Amorites, a chief Canaanite people, whose iniquity was not yet full, v.16, the Canaanites, the people of the land generally, or a particular tribe, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites, the people of Jebus/Jerusalem, the nations to be dispossessed by Israel); the Amorites (named in v.16, whose iniquity would in time be full) a chief and powerful Canaanite nation, sometimes standing for the Canaanites generally (the people whose ripened wickedness would bring God's judgment by Israel's conquest); the ten nations (vv.19-21) — Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaim, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, Jebusites — the peoples whose land was granted by covenant to Abram's seed (the full extent of the promised inheritance, the nations Israel would, in part, dispossess, the covenant of the land sealed with the naming of its inhabitants) — Gill.

themes: And the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Girgashites, and the Jebusites
the completing of the list of the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed
the Amorites, whose iniquity would in time be full (v.16), a chief Canaanite people
the ten nations, the full extent of the covenant-grant of the land
